docs.title: Calculators
docs.text:
Interactive tools for cardiac CT and MRI interpretation — runs locally in your browser, no data sent anywhere.

🫀 ECV Calculator

Extracellular volume fraction from T1 mapping (pre/post gadolinium + hematocrit). Provides partition coefficient and interpretation.

🌊 4D Flow Analysis

Qp:Qs shunt fraction, regurgitant fractions, pulmonary flow split, and BSA-indexed cardiac outputs. Standard, Fontan, and quick modes.

💨 Simplified Bernoulli

Peak pressure gradient from peak velocity. Severity grading by valve. TR jet → RVSP estimation.

docs.title: Simplified Bernoulli
docs.text:
Convert between peak velocity and pressure gradient using the simplified Bernoulli equation.

Formula: \(\Delta P = 4 V_{max}^2\)

Velocity ↔ Pressure Gradient Peak Velocity (m/s) ↔ Pressure Gradient (mmHg)

docs.title: ECV Calculator
docs.text:
Extracellular volume fraction from T1 mapping — requires pre- and post-contrast MOLLI (or ShMOLLI) and hematocrit.

ECV from T1 Mapping

Extracellular volume fraction from pre- and post-contrast MOLLI — requires hematocrit

Hematocrit (%) Pre-Gd T1 — Myo (ms) Pre-Gd T1 — Blood (ms) Post-Gd T1 — Myo (ms) Post-Gd T1 — Blood (ms)

Enter all T1 values and hematocrit to calculate ECV.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
Formula: \(ECV = (1 - Hct) \times \frac{\Delta R1_{myo}}{\Delta R1_{blood}}\) where \(\Delta R1 = \frac{1}{T1_{post}} - \frac{1}{T1_{pre}}\)

ECV (%) Interpretation Clinical Context <30 Normal Normal extracellular matrix 30–32 Borderline Correlate with clinical context 32–40 Elevated Diffuse fibrosis / edema >40 Markedly Elevated Consider amyloid or severe fibrosis

Measurement Tips

  • Use the same 3 slices (base/mid/apex) for pre- and post-contrast MOLLI — identical slice positions matter
  • Blood pool T1 measured from a ROI in the LV cavity (avoid trabeculae)
  • Gadolinium should fully equilibrate before post-Gd MOLLI — ideally ≥10 min post-injection
  • Hematocrit from same-day labs; point-of-care Hct is acceptable
  • At 1.5T: native T1 ~950–1050 ms (myo), ~1600–1750 ms (blood); at 3T: ~1150–1300 ms (myo), ~1800–2000 ms (blood)

docs.title: Cardiac CT
docs.text: Scanning protocols for cardiac CT are maintained in the Radiology Protocol Manager. This page covers general protocol requirements, acquisition considerations, and troubleshooting pearls.

docs.title: CAD-RADS 2.0 Reporting
docs.text:
CAD-RADS Stenosis Recommendation 0 No stenosis No CAD 1 1–24% Minimal, non-obstructive 2 25–49% Mild, non-obstructive 3 50–69% Moderate — functional testing recommended 4A 70–99% (1–2 vessels) Severe — ICA or FFR-CT 4B 70–99% (LM or 3-vessel) Severe — heart team 5 Total occlusion N Non-diagnostic segment

Modifiers: +HRP (high-risk plaque), +S (stent), +G (graft), +E (exception), +I (ischemia)

High-Risk Plaque Features

Low attenuation plaque (<30 HU), positive remodeling (RI >1.1), napkin-ring sign, spotty calcification

docs.title: Motion Artifact
docs.text: Cause Solution Heart rate too high Administer additional beta-blocker; re-scan if feasible Irregular rhythm (AF, ectopy) Switch to retrospective helical with multisegment reconstruction; use wider padding windows Poor breath-hold Shorter breath-hold protocol; supplemental O₂; patient coaching Respiratory motion (diaphragm) Re-scan; increase table speed; adjust breath-hold instruction

docs.title: Contrast Issues
docs.text: Problem Cause Fix Poor aortic enhancement (<250 HU) Mistimed bolus, low output, slow injection Re-time with test bolus; increase injection rate or contrast volume Right heart opacification inadequate (structural CT) Standard left-heart timing Use longer contrast bolus add a delay phase Streak artifact from contrast in SVC Left antecubital access, high concentration in SVC Use right arm; add saline dilution mix; delay scan start Contrast extravasation IV infiltration Abort scan; assess extent; reschedule Contrast reaction Allergy / prior reaction Pre-medicate per institutional protocol; have emergency equipment available

docs.title: Image Quality
docs.text: Problem Likely Cause Solution Blooming from calcium / stents Beam hardening Use sharper reconstruction kernel; increase kV; iterative reconstruction Noise / grainy images Low tube current or high BMI Increase mAs (or kVp); widen gating window; use iterative reconstruction Mis-registration (coronary snap) Variable heart rate during helical scan Retrospective gating with wider reconstruction window; manual phase selection

docs.title: Reporting Checklist
docs.text:
CCTAStructural / Device Planning
  • [ ] Coronary dominance
  • [ ] Proximal, mid, distal segments for each vessel
  • [ ] CAD-RADS classification + modifiers
  • [ ] Non-cardiac findings (lungs, mediastinum, liver)
  • [ ] Confirm adequate phase coverage (retrospective full-cycle)
  • [ ] Confirm adequate right and left heart opacification (if applicable)
  • [ ] Document annular measurements in correct cardiac phase
  • [ ] Assess vascular access route
  • [ ] Note any incidental findings

docs.title: LAAO Planning CT
docs.text: Pre-procedural CT for left atrial appendage occlusion (LAAO). For scan protocols, see the Protocol Manager.

docs.title: LAA Anatomy
docs.text:
Morphologies (clinical relevance):

Morphology Description Notes Chicken wing Sharp bend in proximal LAA More protective against stroke? Cactus Multiple lobes off main trunk Complex — larger device may be needed Windsock Single dominant lobe Common Cauliflower No dominant lobe, irregular Highest stroke risk; most challenging

docs.title: Key Measurements
docs.text: Measurement How Notes LAA ostium max D Widest diameter at ostium Device size = ostium D + 3–6 mm LAA ostium min D Narrowest diameter Elliptical — both dimensions needed LAA ostium area Planimetry Alternative to diameters Landing zone D 1–2 cm from ostium Where device sits LAA depth From ostium to tip (or first bend) Must be ≥ device length LAA to LSPV Relationship — avoid impinging PV

docs.title: Thrombus Protocol
docs.text:
Delayed Phase (60 s)

Residual enhancement on delayed phase effectively excludes thrombus. Absence of fill-in on delayed phase = LAA thrombus → do NOT proceed to closure until resolved.

docs.title: Watchman Device Sizing
docs.text: Scenario Rule Max LAA ostium D ≤31.5 mm Standard Watchman FLX Max LAA ostium D >31.5 mm Larger device or alternative Device size Max ostium D + 3–6 mm (8–20% compression target)

docs.title: TAVR Planning CT
docs.text: Pre-procedural CT is the essential imaging modality for TAVR candidate assessment. For scan protocols, see the Protocol Manager.

docs.title: Aortic Annulus Sizing
docs.text:
Measure perpendicular to the LVOT outflow axis (coronal oblique through the three hinge points of the aortic cusps). Measure in systole (or the phase with the largest annular area).

Measurement How Notes Perimeter-derived D Trace annular perimeter → D = P/π Primary sizing metric Area-derived D Trace annular area → D = 2√(A/π) Cross-check Max / Min diameter Widest and narrowest annular diameters Assess eccentricity Annular area Planimetry Directly guides device selection LVOT D (3–5 mm below annulus) Perpendicular measurement CoreValve sizing; LVOT obstruction risk

Sizing convention

Area- and perimeter-derived diameters are preferred over simple 2D max/min diameters for device selection. Eccentricity index = (max D − min D) / max D; values >0.10 indicate significant eccentricity.

docs.title: Aortic Root Measurements
docs.text: Level What to Measure Notes Sinus of Valsalva Max width across each cusp (L, R, N) Must accommodate device; typically 27–35 mm Sinotubular junction (STJ) Diameter Narrow STJ constrains self-expanding devices Ascending aorta Max diameter Sheath compatibility; ≥45 mm = surgical threshold (bicuspid) Aortoventricular angle LVOT-to-aorta angulation Affects paravalvular leak risk and optimal fluoroscopic projection Coronary ostial heights LCA and RCA from annular plane See coronary occlusion risk below

docs.title: Coronary Occlusion Risk
docs.text:
Measure coronary ostial height perpendicular to the annular plane.

Coronary Ostial Height Risk Level Action >15 mm Low Standard TAVR planning 10–15 mm Moderate Consider BASILICA; evaluate sinus width <10 mm High BASILICA or alternative approach; Heart Team discussion

Additional factors that increase risk regardless of height: narrow sinus of Valsalva (<30 mm), low STJ, bulky or fused leaflets, valve-in-valve procedures.

Bicuspid Aortic Valve + TAVR

Note raphe type: Sievers 0 (no raphe), Sievers 1 (1 raphe), Sievers 2 (2 raphes). Eccentric or bulky calcification increases paravalvular leak and annular rupture risk. CT calcium scoring and geometric assessment (eccentricity index, calcium distribution) are essential. Coronary occlusion risk may be higher due to cusp fusion patterns.

docs.title: Vascular Access
docs.text:
Access Site Min Diameter Notes CFA (transfemoral) ≥5.5–6.0 mm (device-dependent) Preferred route — assess full iliofemoral runoff Iliofemoral vessels ≥5.5 mm Assess tortuosity, calcification, occlusions Subclavian / axillary ≥6.0 mm (left preferred) Alternative if femoral unfavorable Transaortic Direct ascending aorta access Reserved for prohibitive femoral/subclavian anatomy Transapical Direct LV apex Avoided when possible; only if all others unsuitable

Key measurements: Minimum luminal diameter at each level; iliofemoral angle >90° = higher vascular complication risk; sheath outer diameter vs vessel diameter ratio.

docs.title: Fluoroscopic Projection Planning
docs.text: CT can predict the optimal fluoroscopic working angle (coplanar view) by identifying the plane perpendicular to the aortic annulus. Report LAO/RAO and cranial/caudal angulations to the interventional team.

docs.title: Post-Procedural CT
docs.text: CT is the reference standard for detecting hypo-attenuating leaflet thickening (HALT) and reduced leaflet motion (RELM) post-TAVR. Also essential for valve-in-valve planning (inner diameter of implanted prosthesis, risk of coronary obstruction).

docs.title: References
docs.text: Document Notes Blanke P, et al. "Computed Tomography Imaging in the Context of TAVI/TAVR: An Expert Consensus Document of the SCCT." JACC: Cardiovascular Imaging. 2019;12(1):1–24. DOI: 10.1016/j.jcmg.2018.12.003 Primary reference — current SCCT consensus; annular sizing, protocol, structured reporting, HALT detection Achenbach S, et al. "SCCT Expert Consensus Document on CT Imaging Before TAVI/TAVR." J Cardiovasc Comput Tomogr. 2012;6(6):366–380. DOI: 10.1016/j.jcct.2012.11.002 Original landmark SCCT consensus; established CT as essential modality Francone M, et al. DOI: 10.1007/s00330-019-06357-8 Radiology-centric complement; protocol harmonization across scanner platforms; structured reporting for Heart Valve Team

docs.title: TMVR Planning CT
docs.text:
CT is central to TMVR candidate selection and procedure planning, with neo-LVOT area serving as the gatekeeper measurement for procedural feasibility. For scan protocols, see the Protocol Manager.

Acquisition

Retrospective ECG-gated full-cycle multiphase dataset required (typically 5–10% R-R intervals) to enable neo-LVOT assessment across cardiac phases and select the optimal sizing phase (end-systole for annulus; multiphase for neo-LVOT simulation).

docs.title: Mitral Annulus Sizing
docs.text:
The mitral annulus is saddle-shaped and D-shaped — measure in end-systole when the annulus is smallest and most reproducible.

Measurement How Annular perimeter Trace the full hinge-point ring in systole using sagittal + coronal oblique planes Anteroposterior (AP) diameter Mid-point from anterior mitral valve leaflet (AMVL) to posterior mitral valve leaflet (PMVL) insertion Mediolateral (ML) diameter Intercommissural distance Annular area Planimetry in reformatted plane perpendicular to the annular ring Annular height Septo-aortic horn (SAo) to inferior ventricular (IVt) commissural plane

Saddle shape

The mitral annulus has two high points (anteroseptal and posterolateral) and two low points (intercommissural). Perimeter- and area-derived sizing are preferred; 2D diameters alone underestimate true dimensions.

docs.title: Neo-LVOT Assessment
docs.text:
Critical Threshold: ≤1.7 cm² = High Risk

Neo-LVOT area is the single most important CT-derived parameter for TMVR feasibility. A predicted neo-LVOT area ≤1.7 cm² (170 mm²) carries high risk for dynamic LVOT obstruction and hemodynamic compromise.

Simulation method:

  1. Place a virtual device (cylinder matching prosthesis dimensions) at the annular plane on CT
  2. Reformat at end-systole — the phase when the AMVL reaches maximal systolic anterior motion
  3. Measure the residual triangular orifice bounded by: virtual device body, AMVL, and interventricular septum (IVS)
  4. Report minimum neo-LVOT area
Neo-LVOT Area Risk Considerations >2.0 cm² Lower risk Proceed with standard planning 1.7–2.0 cm² Intermediate Careful assessment; consider multiphase analysis ≤1.7 cm² High risk Consider LAMPOON, alcohol septal ablation, or alternate therapy

Risk varies by scenario:

Clinical Scenario Neo-LVOT Risk Profile Native TMVR Variable; depends on AMVL length and IVS thickness Valve-in-valve (prior surgical bioprosthesis) Generally lower — stented frame constrains leaflets Valve-in-ring (prior annuloplasty ring) Higher — ring constrains annular dilatation, AMVL may be long Valve-in-MAC (mitral annular calcification) Often highest — calcium bulk displaces device anteriorly

docs.title: Adjacent Structures
docs.text: Structure What to Assess Circumflex artery Distance from annular plane; risk of compression by device Aortic valve Aortomitral continuity angle; anterior displacement risk Left atrial appendage Relationship to device; LAA closure feasibility Pulmonary veins Proximity to device landing zone

docs.title: Transseptal Planning
docs.text: Feature Target / Measurement Fossa ovalis thickness <4 mm for unobstructed transseptal puncture Transseptal height above annular plane 3.5–4.5 cm (device delivery trajectory) Interatrial septum-to-annulus angle Determines catheter trajectory and device alignment Left atrial dimensions Adequate chamber size for device delivery system

docs.title: Fluoroscopic Projection Planning
docs.text: CT-derived coplanar angle prediction: the plane perpendicular to the mitral annular plane defines the optimal fluoroscopic working view. Report LAO/RAO and cranial/caudal angulations.

docs.text: Document Notes Blanke P, et al. "Multimodality Imaging in the Context of Transcatheter Mitral Valve Replacement: Establishing Consensus Among Modalities and Disciplines." JACC: Cardiovascular Imaging. 2015;8(10):1191–1208. DOI: 10.1016/j.jcmg.2015.08.004 Foundational consensus — standardized framework for CT-based mitral annular segmentation, sizing algorithms, neo-LVOT concept, fluoroscopic angle prediction Reid A, et al. docs.title: TTVR Planning CT
docs.text:
CT planning for transcatheter tricuspid valve interventions is the least standardized of the three transcatheter valve domains. The tricuspid annulus is larger, more dynamic, more non-planar, and more anatomically variable than the mitral annulus, and right-heart contrast opacification presents unique protocol challenges. For scan protocols, see the Protocol Manager.

Acquisition Requirements

ECG-triggered multiphasic dataset: reconstruct at 5–10% R-R intervals with ≤1 mm slice thickness. Right-heart–optimized contrast protocol required — standard left-heart protocols produce inadequate opacification of the right atrium, annulus, and subvalvular apparatus. Consider split-bolus or modified injection timing to simultaneously opacify right and left heart chambers.

docs.title: Tricuspid Annulus Sizing
docs.text:
The tricuspid annulus is non-planar and highly dynamic — dimensions vary substantially across the cardiac cycle. Measure in end-systole for sizing.

Measurement Notes Annular perimeter Primary sizing metric; trace full hinge-point ring in systole Anteroposterior (AP) diameter Typically 40–55 mm; largest anterior-posterior dimension Mediolateral (ML) diameter Intercommissural (septal-to-lateral) distance Annular area Planimetry in best-fit plane perpendicular to annular ring Annular height / non-planarity Saddle shape — measure deviation from flat plane Coaptation gap Distance between leaflet free edges in systole; relevant for edge-to-edge repair sizing

docs.title: Adjacent Structures
docs.text:
Right-heart anatomy is critical for safe device delivery and anchoring.

Structure What to Assess Right coronary artery (RCA) Distance from annular plane — RCA runs in the AV groove adjacent to the tricuspid annulus; compression risk with annuloplasty devices and replacement prostheses Papillary muscles Position and number of subvalvular papillary muscles; relevant for device anchoring and chordal preservation Right atrium Dimensions and morphology; large RA common in severe TR Right ventricle RV volume (RVEDVi), wall thickness, systolic function; severely dilated RV may impair device anchoring Coronary sinus Position relative to annulus; relevant for annuloplasty ring and some replacement systems Atrioventricular node Septal location — conduction risk with device implantation near the septum

docs.title: Vascular Access and Delivery Routes
docs.text:
Access Route Relevant Measurements Notes Transfemoral (venous) IVC diameter; IVC-to-annulus distance; IVC angulation Most common; large-bore venous sheaths (24–36 Fr) Transjugular Jugular-to-annulus distance; SVC-to-RA-to-annulus angle Used for some annuloplasty and replacement systems Subclavian / axillary (venous) Vessel diameter; angulation to RA Alternative venous access Transatrial / direct RA Surgical hybrid approach Less common

Measure IVC diameter at multiple levels; assess for IVC filter (may preclude femoral access); evaluate hepatic vein anatomy (relevant for caval valve implantation).

docs.title: Device Class–Specific Parameters
docs.text:
Different device classes require different CT measurements:

Device Class Key CT Parameters Edge-to-edge repair (e.g., TRILUMINATE, CLASP) Coaptation gap, leaflet length, annular dimensions, RV geometry Annuloplasty devices Annular perimeter, RCA proximity, coronary sinus position Orthotopic replacement Full annular sizing (perimeter, area, diameters), subvalvular anatomy, RV size, access route Caval valve implantation (e.g., TRICENTO, CAVI) IVC and SVC diameters at multiple levels; hepatic vein anatomy

docs.title: Fluoroscopic Projection Planning
docs.text: CT can predict the optimal coplanar fluoroscopic working angle perpendicular to the tricuspid annular plane. Report LAO/RAO and cranial/caudal angulations; the tricuspid annulus is typically viewed in a right anterior oblique projection.

docs.text: Document Notes Hell MM, et al. "Computed Tomography Imaging Needs for Novel Transcatheter Tricuspid Valve Repair and Replacement Therapies." European Heart Journal – Cardiovascular Imaging. 2021;22(6):601–610. DOI: 10.1093/ehjci/jeaa308 Most widely cited dedicated review — CT acquisition protocols with right-heart optimization, annular measurements, RCA proximity, access route assessment, fluoroscopic projection; organized by device class Hausleiter J, et al. docs.title: Cardiac MRI — Overview & Sequences
docs.text: Overview of commonly used cardiac MRI sequences, their key purposes, and acquisition parameters.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Cardiac Anesthesia

  • 4 cardiac issues that will make anesthetists stressed if you ask them to do a study under GA:
  • Severe aortic stenosis
  • Pulmonary Hypertension
  • Severe LV heart failure
  • Arrhythmia (ventricular)

Motion Artifacts (ghosting)

  • Arrhythmia: switch to real-time cine (lower resolution but no ECG issues)
  • Breathing: use free-breathing with navigator; check patient coaching
  • Cine ghosting: check VENC if using velocity-encoded sequence; check shimming

LGE — Wrong TI

Always run a TI scout after gadolinium dosing. Re-run if myocardium not nulled (appears bright on PSIR inversion images).

Banding Artifacts (bSSFP)

Banding from B₀ inhomogeneity: re-shim, or switch to 1.5T equivalent (spoiled GRE / FIESTA at off-resonance range). More problematic at 3T.

4D Flow Quality Check

  • Velocity aliasing: increase VENC (but SNR decreases)
  • Flow inconsistencies: SVC + IVC should ≈ PA; Ao ≈ PA in normal hearts
  • Check background phase correction in post-processing software

docs.title: Standard Views
docs.text: View Prescription Content 4-chamber (4CH) From axial showing RA/LA/RV/LV All four chambers; mitral and tricuspid valves 2-chamber (2CH) Perpendicular to 4CH through LV LA, LV, mitral valve 3-chamber (3CH) LVOT view LV, LVOT, aortic valve; rules out LVOTO Short axis (SAX) Perpendicular to LV long axis Stack base to apex for volumetry

docs.title: Cardiac MRI Protocols
docs.text:
Overview of standard cardiac MRI protocols and the sequences used in each.

Protocol Primary Indication Key Sequences Cardiomyopathy HF, VT, infiltrative disease, cardiomyopathy evaluation Cine SSFP (SAX + LAX), LGE, T1 mapping (ECV), T2 mapping Stress Perfusion Suspected CAD, ischemia evaluation Rest + stress perfusion, LGE Congenital / 4D Flow CHD, shunt quantification, flow analysis Dark blood morphology, cine, 4D flow or 2D PC Cardiac Masses Cardiac tumor, thrombus, pericardial mass Multi-echo T1, T2, perfusion, LGE

docs.title: Contrast Doses
docs.text:
Use Dose Agent Standard LGE / perfusion 0.1 mmol/kg (single) Gadolinium-based (macrocyclic preferred) Perfusion + LGE same study 0.1 mmol/kg for perfusion; additional 0.05–0.1 for LGE if needed — T1 mapping (ECV) 0.1 mmol/kg Record pre-contrast T1 first

NSF Risk

Use macrocyclic agents (Group II) for patients with eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m². Avoid linear agents. Discuss risk/benefit if eGFR <15 or on dialysis.

docs.title: Cardiac Masses Protocol
docs.text: Indications: Suspected cardiac tumor, thrombus, pericardial mass, vegetation (supplementary to echo/CT).

docs.title: Sequence Options
docs.text:
General Considerations - Size: Masses < 1 cm are difficult to see. - Location: Document where the mass is located. - Imaging Planes: Include Standard Views AND Oblique Views to better visualize the mass.

# Sequence View Notes 1 Localizers 3-plane 2 Cine SSFP (or GRE backup) Standard + Oblique Mass mobility, attachment, function 3 T1 DIRFSE 2 planes Required: At least one in two planes 4 T1 DIRFSE FS 2 planes Required: At least one in two planes 5 T2 DIRFSE FS 2 planes Required: At least one in two planes 6 T1 TIRFSE Targeted Optional 7 Tagging Targeted Optional 8 Parametric mapping Targeted Optional — — — Inject gadolinium 9 First pass perfusion Targeted Especially if thrombus/hemangioma suspected 10 T1 pre/post LAVA/T1G Targeted 11 T1 DIRFSE FS (Post) Targeted 12 EGE Targeted Early Gadolinium Enhancement 13 LGE Targeted Late Gadolinium Enhancement

docs.title: Example Protocol
docs.text: # Sequence View Notes 1 Localizers 3-plane 2 Cine SSFP 4CH, 2CH, 3CH, SAX, targeted views Mass mobility, attachment, valve obstruction, chamber function 3 T2 STIR Targeted views High signal in hemangioma, cyst, malignant masses 4 T1 w/o fat sat Targeted views Detect T1 bright signal (fat, thrombus, hemorrhage) 5 T1 + fat sat Same views Fat suppression confirms lipomatous lesion — — Inject gadolinium (0.1 mmol/kg) 6 Dynamic perfusion Targeted Enhancement kinetics — perfused vs. avascular 7 TI Scout — 8 LGE SAX stack + targeted mass views Delayed enhancement pattern

docs.title: Tissue Characteristics by CMR
docs.text:
Feature Low T1 High T1 Low T2 High T2 Enhances Thrombus ✓ (acute) ✓ (subacute/chronic) ✓ ✗ No (avascular) Myxoma ✓ ✗ ✗ ✓ Yes (heterogeneous) Lipoma ✗ ✓ (fat) ✗ ✓ No Fibroelastoma ✓ ✗ ✗ ✗ Variable Hemangioma ✓ ✗ ✗ ✓ ✓ Yes (early, intense) Angiosarcoma Variable ✗ ✗ ✓ Yes (heterogeneous, nodular) Metastasis Variable Variable Variable Variable Yes Pericardial cyst ✓ ✗ ✗ ✓ ✓ No

Thrombus Key Feature

Thrombus does not enhance on perfusion or delayed LGE. A perfused mass enhances — not a thrombus.

docs.title: Benign
docs.text: Mass Location Characteristics Myxoma LA (fossa ovalis) 75%, RA 15% Heterogeneous, mobile, jelly-like; T2 bright, variable enhancement Lipoma Anywhere (RA most common) Homogeneous T1 bright, suppressed on fat sat Papillary fibroelastoma Valve leaflets Tiny, mobile; best seen on echo; low T1/T2, poor CMR visualization Hemangioma RV free wall Very T2 bright; early intense enhancement Rhabdomyoma Ventricular myocardium Infants; multiple; tuberous sclerosis Fibroma IVS / LV free wall T1 iso, T2 dark, intense LGE (dense fibrous tissue) Pericardial cyst Right costophrenic angle Simple cyst; T2 bright, no enhancement

docs.title: Malignant
docs.text: Mass Location Characteristics Angiosarcoma RA, pericardium Nodular, hemorrhagic, T2 bright; "sunray" appearance; aggressive enhancement Undifferentiated sarcoma LA (mimics myxoma) Invasion, heterogeneous enhancement Lymphoma Pericardium, myocardium Diffuse infiltration; preserves anatomy Metastasis Any chamber / pericardium History of primary malignancy; lung, breast, melanoma most common Mesothelioma Pericardium Diffuse pericardial involvement

docs.title: Cardiomyopathy Protocol
docs.text: Indications: Ischemic and non-ischemic cardiomyopathy characterization (HCM, DCM, myocarditis, infiltrative), ECV calculation.

docs.title: Sequence Order
docs.text: # Sequence View Notes 1 Localizers 3-plane Quick survey 2 Dark blood HASTE Axial stack (thorax) Morphology survey; pericardium; pleura 3 Cine SSFP 4CH Can do as a stack for ARVC 4 Cine SSFP 2CH LV function, mitral 5 Cine SSFP 3CH LVOT, mitral, aortic valve, can do as a stack for HOCM 6 Cine SSFP SAX stack (base → apex in 8 mm steps) Volumetry 7 T2 DIR 3 SAX (base/mid/apex) 8 T2 mapping 3 SAX (base/mid/apex) optional, helpful in myocarditis 9 T1 MOLLI (pre-contrast) 3 SAX (base/mid/apex) — — Inject gadolinium (0.1 mmol/kg) Note time and dose 10 TI Scout Mid-SA Determine TI to null normal myocardium 11 LGE SAX stack Adjusted TI from scout, single-shot first, then repeat without single shot 12 LGE 4CH, 2CH, 3CH Orthogonal views 13 T1 MOLLI (post-contrast) SAX Calculate ECV

docs.title: Optional Sequences
docs.text: # Sequence View Notes 1 Cine SSFP RV2CH/RV3CH/RVOT RV morphology and function 2 Cine SSFP Aortic valve (en face) Valve morphology, orifice 3 Cine SSFP SAX Free breathing For pericardial constriction

docs.title: Troubleshooting Tips
docs.text:
Cine SSFP - If gating issues, switch to cineDL (less sensitive to heart rate changes) - if banding artifacts, switch to GRE or cineDL - For small banding artifacts overlying portion of the myocardium (typically at lung interface), can try changing center frequency. - GRE should be done post-contrast

LGE - Try to get additional slices to confirm LGE if seen at the scanner. - If difficult with breath-hold, try single shot LGE. - If still not working, do cine IRs in multiple planes. - Can use raw MOLLI sequences as a double check for LGE.

docs.title: LGE Patterns and Diagnosis
docs.text:
Pattern Distribution Likely Diagnosis Subendocardial / transmural Coronary territory Ischemic scar Mid-wall Septal (IVS) "stripe" DCM, myocarditis, LBBB Subepicardial Lateral wall Myocarditis (focal) Diffuse subendocardial Global, circumferential Amyloid; also consider ECV RV insertion points Bilateral septal junctions Pressure overload (pulm HTN, TOF) Patchy / geographic HCM segments HCM — correlates with arrhythmia risk Pericardium Delayed enhancement Pericarditis

Ischemic vs. Non-Ischemic

Ischemic scar always starts at the endocardium and may extend transmurally. Non-ischemic patterns (mid-wall, subepicardial, RV insertion) are never endocardial-dominant.

docs.title: ECV Calculation
docs.text:
Formula: \(ECV = (1 - Hct) \times \frac{\Delta R1_{myo}}{\Delta R1_{blood}}\)

Where \(\Delta R1 = \frac{1}{T1_{post}} - \frac{1}{T1_{pre}}\) (in ms⁻¹)

Use the ECV Calculator on the Calculators page.

ECV (%) Interpretation <25 Normal 25–30 Borderline 30–40 Diffuse fibrosis / edema >40 Markedly elevated — amyloid?

Amyloid Pattern

High native T1, high ECV, diffuse subendocardial LGE, difficulty nulling myocardium, small LV with thick walls + preserved/reduced EF. RV involvement common.

docs.title: Congenital / 4D Flow Protocol
docs.text:
Indications: Congenital heart disease (ASD, VSD, TOF, TGA, Fontan, coarctation), shunt quantification, valvular flow (AR/PR from CMR), vessel stenosis assessment.

See the Congenital Heart Disease section for more information.

docs.title: Sequence Order
docs.text:
# Sequence View Notes 1 Localizers 3-plane 2 Dark blood HASTE Axial stack (thorax) Situs, connections, great vessel relationships 3 Dark blood HASTE Coronal / oblique sagittal Aortic arch, PA, SVC/IVC anatomy 4 Cine SSFP 4CH, 2CH, 3CH LV/RV function; AV valve regurgitation 5 Cine SSFP SAX stack (LV) LV volumetry and wall motion 6 Cine SSFP RV3CH/RV2Ch/RVOT optional for TOF, right heart lesions 7 2D Phase Contrast Perpendicular to Ao, PA, LPA, RPA Optional if doing 4D flow; Flow quantification; Qp:Qs 8 4D Flow Volume covering heart and great vessels Replaces 2D PC if available; post-process for all vessels 9 CE MRA Sagittal Anatomy, stenosis

2D PC vs. 4D Flow

2D PC is faster and works well when you know the specific vessels to measure. 4D flow allows retrospective plane placement and WSS/visualization — better for complex anatomy or when vessels are uncertain.

docs.title: Phase Contrast (2D PC) — Practical Tips
docs.text:
• VENC: Set 10–20% above expected peak velocity. Aliasing artifacts = increase VENC. Too high VENC = poor SNR. See Bernoulli Calculator for more information.
  • Typical: Ao ~150–180 cm/s, PA ~100–130 cm/s, SVC ~60–80 cm/s
  • Plane placement: Perpendicular to vessel long axis; avoid turbulence zones (place 1–2 cm from valve)
  • Background phase correction: Apply in post-processing if available (e.g., use stationary tissue outside vessel)

docs.title: Qp:Qs Calculation
docs.text:
Standard: \(\frac{Qp}{Qs} = \frac{\text{PA net flow}}{\text{Ao net flow}}\)

If LPA + RPA measured: use sum as Qp.

Use the 4D Flow Calculator on the Calculators page.

docs.title: Key Reporting Elements
docs.text:
• Situs and connections: Situs (solitus/inversus/ambiguous), visceroatrial concordance, AV and ventriculoarterial concordance
  • Shunts: Qp:Qs, shunt direction, size of defect on cine
  • Biventricular function: EDVi, EF for both ventricles (BSA-indexed)
  • Flow measurements: Net flows (mL/beat) for Ao, PA, LPA, RPA; RF for pulmonary regurgitation
  • Stenosis: Gradient by modified Bernoulli from peak PC velocity
  • LPA:RPA ratio: Normal ~55:45; >60:40 = asymmetric flow (branch PA stenosis)
  • Collateral flow (Fontan): Qp − Fontan flow; >20% suggests significant aortopulmonary collaterals

docs.title: Stress Perfusion Protocol
docs.text:
Indications: Known or suspected CAD, ischemia evaluation (alternative to nuclear), pre/post revascularization assessment.

Stress agents: Adenosine (140 mcg/kg/min × 4–6 min), Regadenoson (0.4 mg bolus), Dobutamine (10–40 mcg/kg/min — for bSSFP cine stress wall motion if vasodilator contraindicated)

docs.title: Contraindications to Vasodilator Stress
docs.text:
Contraindications

  • Active bronchospasm / severe asthma
  • High-degree AV block (2° or 3°) without pacemaker
  • Systolic BP <90 mmHg
  • Dipyridamole within 24 h (potentiates adenosine)
  • Caffeine within 12 h (blocks adenosine receptors — hold coffee day of scan)

docs.title: Sequence Order
docs.text:
# Sequence View Notes 1 Localizers 3-plane 2 Dark blood HASTE Axial Morphology 3 Cine SSFP 4CH, 2CH, 3CH, SAX Baseline function / wall motion 4 Rest Perfusion 3 SAX (base/mid/apex) 5 Rest cines (repeat if wall motion question) SAX Optional 6 — Begin stress agent infusion Allow 2–4 min before stress imaging 7 Stress Perfusion Same 3 SAX slices At peak stress; same contrast dose 8 Wait 10–15 min — Gadolinium equilibrates 9 TI Scout Mid-SA 10 LGE SAX stack + LAX Scar assessment

Contrast Dose

Use half-dose (0.05–0.075 mmol/kg) per perfusion acquisition to avoid T2* saturation artifact. Some protocols use full dose (0.1) — be consistent with your institution.

docs.title: Normal vs. Abnormal
docs.text:
• Normal: Uniform signal uptake in myocardium following gadolinium bolus. LV myocardium brightens uniformly from endo to epi.
  • Perfusion defect: Subendocardial or transmural dark region on first-pass images, corresponding to territory of stenosed vessel.
  • Dark rim artifact: Thin (1 pixel) subendocardial dark rim — common, especially at base. Not a true defect if limited to 1 slice and no corresponding LGE.

docs.title: Coronary Territory
docs.text: Territory Coronary Artery Anterior, anteroseptal LAD Lateral, posterolateral LCx Inferior, inferoseptal RCA (right dominant) Inferior lateral LCx (left dominant) Septal perforators LAD proximal

docs.title: Ischemia vs. Scar
docs.text: Finding Interpretation Stress defect + No LGE Ischemia (viable, stunned, or hibernating myocardium) — candidate for revascularization Stress defect + LGE (>50% transmural) Fixed scar — revascularization unlikely to help Stress defect + LGE (<50% transmural) Mixed — some viable myocardium No stress defect + No LGE Normal — no significant ischemia or scar

docs.title: Congenital Heart Disease
docs.text:
CMR reporting template and reference for imaging pearls in common CHD entities.

Normal TOF TGA ccTGA DORV ASD VSD Fontan CoA Ebstein AVSD Segmental Analysis Template 📋 Copy Template

Situs: Situs solitus by normal spleen in the left upper quadrant and normal pulmonary arteries and airways relationships.

Cavae: Single right-sided SVC and single right-sided IVC drain to the right atrium unobstructed.

Pulmonary Veins: Two right-sided pulmonary vein(s) and Two left-sided pulmonary vein(s) drain to the left atrium. No evidence of compression, stenosis or anomalous pulmonary venous return.

Atria: No gross interatrial communication.

Atrioventricular Connection: Concordant. An endocardial cushion and two distinct atrioventricular valves are present.

Ventricles: D-loop with levocardia. Both ventricles are normal in size, morphology and function. No gross interventricular communication.

Ventriculoarterial Connection: Concordant. Main pulmonary artery courses anterior and to the left of the ascending aorta, consistent with normal position of the great arteries.

Coronary Arteries: Right and left coronary arteries originate from their expected sinuses of Valsalva.

Aorta: Left-sided aortic arch with normal branching pattern. No evidence of aneurysm, coarctation or major aorto-pulmonary collateral arteries.

Pulmonary Arteries: Central pulmonary arteries are patent. No evidence of dilatation or stenosis.

Miscellaneous: None.

Using the Template

Click on any lesion tab below to automatically update the template with lesion-specific findings. The template will highlight key anatomic features for each CHD entity. Click the Copy Template button to copy the formatted text to your clipboard.

docs.title: Individual Lesion Pages
docs.text:
• Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF)
  • Transposition of the Great Arteries (TGA)
  • Congenitally Corrected TGA (ccTGA)
  • Double Outlet Right Ventricle (DORV)
  • Atrial Septal Defect (ASD)
  • Ventricular Septal Defect (VSD)
  • Fontan Circulation
  • Coarctation of the Aorta (CoA)
  • Ebstein Anomaly
  • Atrioventricular Septal Defect (AVSD)

docs.title: General MRI Tips
docs.text:
General Approach

  1. Morphology first — dark blood (HASTE/TSE) axial stack for situs, connections, great vessel relationships
  2. Cine function — SAX stack of each ventricle (measure volumes separately)
  3. Flow — 2D PC or 4D flow at Ao, PA, LPA, RPA, any shunt vessel
  4. LGE — assess fibrosis, especially in systemic RV patients
  5. T1/T2 mapping — if myocarditis, infiltrative, or congestive hepatopathy suspected

Breath-holding: Use free-breathing with respiratory navigator for uncooperative patients or complex anatomy requiring long sequences.

Sedation/anesthesia: Required for young children; coordinate with anesthesia for timing of sequences.

docs.title: Classification
docs.text: Type Prevalence Key Features Secundum 70% Central defect in fossa ovalis; most common Primum 15% Lower, involves AV valve tissue; part of AVSD spectrum Sinus venosus 10% Defect near SVC or IVC; often with anomalous PV return Coronary sinus 5% Rare; drain to coronary sinus

docs.title: Hemodynamic Significance
docs.text:
Shunt magnitude from 4D Flow: - Qp:Qs <1.5: Small defect, typically asymptomatic; closure not routinely indicated - Qp:Qs 1.5–2.0: Moderate; warrants monitoring - Qp:Qs >2.0: Large; generally indicated for closure to prevent RV dilation and late complications

Volume overload signs: - RV dilation - RA enlargement - LV compression (RV dilation in diastole)

docs.title: 4D Flow Assessment
docs.text:
• Qp:Qs calculation: Pulmonary artery net flow vs aortic net flow
  • Shunt location: 4D flow can visualize the actual defect and shunt vector
  • Anomalous pulmonary venous return: Especially in sinus venosus ASD (right upper PV commonly drains to SVC)

docs.title: Closure Consideration
docs.text:
Sizing for transcatheter closure: - TEE provides stretched balloon diameter (hemodynamic) - CT/CMR provides static anatomic measurements of rims and defect diameter - Adequate rims needed for device stability (usually >5 mm)

Surgical closure: If anatomy unsuitable for device (rims too short, multiple defects, or PV anomaly requiring surgical correction)

docs.title: Long-term Follow-up
docs.text: Post-closure assessment by echocardiography or cardiac CT to ensure device position and assess for residual shunting.

docs.title: Anatomy
docs.text:
Hallmark: Deficiency of the atrioventricular septum resulting in: - Primum atrial septal defect: Opening between atria - Inlet ventricular septal defect: Opening between ventricles - Common atrioventricular valve: Single valve tissue bridging both atria and ventricles

Associated features: - Left AV valve cleft: Separation between left superior and inferior bridging leaflets - Goose-neck deformity: Elongation and narrowing of left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) - Anterior aortic position: Relative to posteriorly positioned pulmonary artery

docs.title: Classification
docs.text: Type Features Partial AVSD (Ostium primum ASD) Primum ASD only; individual AV valves present; cleft in MV common Intermediate AVSD Small inlet VSD; common AV valve tissue above & below Complete AVSD Large primum ASD + inlet VSD; single common AV valve Rastelli types A (symmetric), B (asymmetric), C (asymmetric with displacement)

docs.title: Hemodynamics
docs.text:
Left-to-right shunting: - Atrial level (primum ASD) - Ventricular level (inlet VSD) - Magnitude depends on defect size and relative AV valve insufficiency

Associated pulmonary hypertension: - Common in complete AVSD if unrepaired - Contributes to progressive left AV valve regurgitation

docs.title: Morphology
docs.text:
• Defect extent: Dark blood imaging (HASTE/TSE) axial and coronal views delineate primum ASD and inlet VSD
  • Common AV valve assessment: Identify single vs cleft valve
  • LVOT narrowing: Sagittal views to assess goose-neck deformity and degree of left AV valve override

docs.title: Ventricular Function
docs.text:
• LV volume and EF: Secondary LV dilation from ASD and VSD
  • RV volume and EF: RV dilation from atrial-level shunting
  • Septation: Position of bridging leaflets at AV junction level

docs.title: AV Valve Regurgitation
docs.text:
• Left AV valve: Most commonly insufficient; quantify by 4D flow
  • Right AV valve: May have cleft; assess competence
  • Severity scoring: Mild, moderate, severe regurgitation

docs.title: Flow Assessment
docs.text:
• 4D flow: Quantify shunt magnitude (Qp:Qs)
  • Bidirectional flow at defects: If pulmonary hypertension present

docs.title: Associated Findings
docs.text:
• Down syndrome: Present in ~25% of patients with AVSD
  • Heterotaxy: Disorders of situs may coexist (laterality issues)
  • Other congenital lesions: TOF, hypoplastic left heart features may accompany

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
• Significant shunting: Qp:Qs >1.5 in partial AVSD, virtually all complete AVSD
  • Symptomatic patients: Poor growth, recurrent infections
  • Pulmonary hypertension prevention: Earlier repair if risk factors

docs.title: Technique
docs.text:
• Primary closure of primum ASD
  • Closure of inlet VSD
  • Reconstruction of cleft left AV valve
  • Maintain separate AV valve orifices

docs.title: Post-repair Complications
docs.text:
• Left AV valve regurgitation: May progress despite surgical repair
  • Can necessitate valve replacement later in life
  • Subaortic stenosis: Residual LVOT obstruction from goose-neck anatomy
  • Arrhythmias: Heart block risk (proximity to conduction tissue)
  • AV valve insufficiency recurrence: Valve leaflet degeneration

docs.title: Imaging Follow-up
docs.text: Serial CMR and/or echocardiography to monitor: - Ventricular volume and function - Residual shunting - Left AV valve regurgitation progression - LVOT obstruction (if present) - Arrhythmia development

docs.title: Congenitally Corrected Transposition of the Great Arteries (ccTGA)
Overview
docs.text: Congenitally corrected transposition (also called L-transposition or L-TGA) is characterized by discordant atrioventricular and discordant ventriculoarterial connections. Despite the abnormal morphology, the physiology is "corrected" at rest because systemic venous return ultimately reaches the lungs, and pulmonary venous return reaches the systemic circulation—however, the systemic right ventricle is at increased risk for ventricular dysfunction over time.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Concordance paradox: Two levels of discordance ("double discordance") result in concordant physiology
  • L-loop ventricles: Right-sided morphologic left ventricle (subpulmonary), left-sided morphologic right ventricle (systemic)
  • Levocardia (normally positioned heart) with inverted chambering
  • Associated lesions very common: VSD, left AV valve regurgitation (systemic mitral valve), Ebstein-like tricuspid deformity, subaortic stenosis, conduction abnormalities

docs.title: Situs and Orientation
docs.text:
• Usually situs solitus but heterotaxy is common
  • Levocardia with normal cardiac position despite inverted chamber arrangement
  • May be associated with other situs abnormalities—evaluate carefully

docs.title: Venous Drainage
docs.text:
• Systemic venous return (SVC + IVC) drains to morphologic right atrium (on right side)
  • Pulmonary venous return drains to morphologic left atrium (on left side)
  • Anomalous pulmonary venous return is frequently associated—check all four pulmonary veins

docs.title: Atrioventricular Connections (Discordant)
docs.text:
• Morphologic right atrium → mitral valve → morphologic left ventricle (subpulmonary, in right position)
  • Morphologic left atrium → tricuspid valve → morphologic right ventricle (systemic, in left position)
  • Systemic mitral valve regurgitation is common (acts as systemic AV valve)
  • Tricuspid valve abnormalities: Ebstein-like malposition with apical displacement and dysplasia

docs.title: Ventricles
docs.text:
• Morphologic left ventricle (subpulmonary):
  • Small, compact morphology
  • In right position (appears as "RV" on echocardiography)

  • Gives rise to pulmonary artery

  • Morphologic right ventricle (systemic):

  • Dilated, thin-walled, less compliant
  • In left position (appears as "LV" on echocardiography)
  • Supports systemic circulation

  • Progressive dilation and dysfunction common in adulthood

  • Ventricular septal defect: Present in ~70–80% of ccTGA cases

  • Usually perimembranous or subarterial
  • May partially restrict left-to-right blood flow

docs.title: Ventriculoarterial Connections (Discordant)
docs.text:
• Morphologic left ventricle (subpulmonary) → pulmonary artery (posteriorly positioned, arises from right-sided ventricle)
  • Morphologic right ventricle (systemic) → aorta (anteriorly positioned, arises from left-sided ventricle)
  • Great arteries typically anterior–posterior relationship or side-by-side

docs.title: Coronary Arteries
docs.text:
• Mirror-image coronary pattern is typical
  • Right coronary artery arises from left-facing sinus (anterior)
  • Left coronary system arises from right-facing sinus (posterior)
  • Coronary ostia may be higher or in abnormal positions
  • Critical for surgical planning (during potential future procedures)

docs.title: Aorta
docs.text:
• Arises from morphologic right ventricle (systemic, left-sided)
  • Typically posterior and to the left relative to pulmonary artery
  • Monitor for:
  • Aortic root dilation
  • Aortic regurgitation (related to VSD anatomy or endocarditis risk)

docs.title: Pulmonary Arteries
docs.text:
• Branch pulmonary arteries usually patent and normally sized
  • May show reduced flow if severe subaortic stenosis (left ventricular outflow tract obstruction)

docs.title: Associated Lesions
docs.text: Lesion Frequency Clinical Significance VSD 70–80% Permits left-to-right shunt; position determines surgical approach Pulmonary stenosis 25–30% Variable; worsens systemic RV afterload Subaortic stenosis 10–20% LVOT obstruction; may require surgical relief Left AV valve regurgitation 40–50% Increases systemic RV volume load; progressive Tricuspid abnormality 10–30% Ebstein-like malposition; worsens TR Conduction abnormalities 50–80% Heart block common; may need pacing; CNS heterotaxy may have situs inversus totalis with right-sided IVC/SVC Heterotaxy/asplenia/polysplenia 5–10% Assess situs carefully

docs.title: Morphologic Assessment
docs.text:
1. Recognizing the inversion:
  2. Use systemic venous return to identify the morphologic right atrium (receives SVC + IVC)
  3. Morphologic ventricle identification: trabeculae, papillary muscles, valve attachments

  4. L-loop: Morphologic LV on right, morphologic RV on left (opposite of normal)

  5. Chamber size and function:

  6. Measure systemic RV volumes and ejection fraction (may be reduced)
  7. Estimate subpulmonary LV size (usually small)

  8. Quantify left (systemic mitral) and right AV valve regurgitation

  9. VSD localization:

  10. Careful cine imaging in multiple planes to define VSD type
  11. Position relative to great arteries (important for operative planning)

docs.title: Flow Imaging
docs.text:
• 4D flow or 2D PC through great arteries to assess:
  • Systemic-to-pulmonary shunt (if VSD present)
  • Systemic AV valve (mitral) regurgitation fraction
  • Subaortic obstruction gradient
  • Branch pulmonary artery flow balance

docs.title: LGE/Fibrosis Assessment
docs.text:
• Assess for myocardial replacement fibrosis in systemic RV (related to chronic volume/pressure overload)
  • Fibrosis correlates with ventricular dysfunction and arrhythmia risk

docs.title: Associated Anomalies
docs.text:
• Pulmonary and systemic venous return anatomy
  • Coronary artery origins and course (may require dedicated coronary CMR protocol)
  • Situs and other congenital associations

docs.title: Uncorrected (Native) ccTGA
docs.text:
• Presentation: Often cyanotic if VSD absent or restrictive; may be acyanotic if large VSD provides adequate mixing
  • Young adulthood: Systemic RV tolerates hemodynamic load reasonably well
  • Progressive dysfunction: 25–30-year-old patients often show declining RV function and increasing valve regurgitation
  • Complications: Arrhythmias, heart failure, endocarditis risk

docs.title: Post-Surgical (if prior intervention)
docs.text:
• VSD closure if hemodynamically significant
  • Pulmonary stenosis relief or RV outflow tract procedures
  • Double switch operation (rare, very young patients): Restores LV as systemic ventricle
  • Atrial switch + arterial switch (historical, less common now)

docs.title: Cardiac
docs.text:
• Conduction abnormalities: ~50% have heart block (related to abnormal AV nodal location)
  • Arrhythmias: Atrial flutter/fibrillation common in older patients; pre-excitation (WPW) possible
  • Endocarditis: Increased risk with VSD and valve abnormalities

docs.title: Extracardiac
docs.text:
• Heterotaxy: Assess splenic position, sidedness of vena cava, lung airway anatomy
  • Cardiac malposition (dextrocardia possible but rare in ccTGA)
  • Associated syndromes: Down syndrome (Trisomy 21)

docs.title: Natural History
docs.text:
• Infants and children often cyanotic; cyanosis may improve with growth or spontaneous closure of PDA
  • Adolescents and young adults generally stable if uncomplicated
  • Systemic RV dysfunction typically emerges in 3rd–4th decade
  • Survival to adulthood without intervention is possible but long-term outcome depends on degree of associated lesions

docs.title: Management Considerations
docs.text:
1. Asymptomatic uncomplicated ccTGA with large VSD:
  2. May not require early intervention

  3. Serial follow-up for RV size and function

  4. Restrictive VSD or cyanosis:

  5. Balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind) in infancy if inadequate mixing

  6. VSD closure if hemodynamically significant

  7. Progressive RV dysfunction, valve regurgitation:

  8. Afterload reduction therapy
  9. Consider double switch operation (controversial; best in young children)

  10. Heart transplantation if end-stage

  11. Endocarditis prophylaxis:

  12. Recommended for patients with VSD or valve abnormalities

docs.title: Key Teaching Points
docs.text: ✓ Two discordances = concordant physiology: Abnormal anatomy delivers blood appropriately but with systemic RV at risk ✓ Mirror-image coronaries: Right coronary from left sinus (anterior) — critical for surgery ✓ Systemic RV dysfunction: The limiting factor; emerges decades into life ✓ High association with VSD, valve regurgitation, subaortic stenosis, and conduction abnormalities ✓ Heterotaxy is frequent: Always assess situs and splenic position

docs.title: References & Further Reading
docs.text:
• Anderson RH, Baker EJ, Macartney FJ, et al. Paediatric cardiology. 3rd ed. Churchill Livingstone; 2010.
  • Fathallah A, Felfli M, Albabtain A. Congenitally corrected transposition of the great arteries: a review of imaging findings and management strategies. Insights Imaging. 2020;11(1):122.
  • Presbitero P, Saracino A, Somerville J. Congenitally corrected transposition of the great arteries. Am J Cardiol. 1995;76(3):153–157.

docs.title: Anatomy
docs.text:
Location: Narrowing of descending thoracic aorta, typically at the isthmus (just distal to left subclavian artery takeoff).

Classification: - Preductal: Proximal to ductus arteriosus (rare; associated with hypoplastic left heart features) - Juxtaductal: At the level of ductus arteriosus (most common) - Postductal: Distal to ductus arteriosus

docs.title: Hemodynamics
docs.text:
• Native anatomy: Narrowing causes proximal hypertension and reduced distal flow
  • Collateral development: Intercostal, internal mammary vessels enlarge to decompress proximal aorta (can be prominent on imaging)
  • Gradient: By PC velocity and Bernoulli equation
  • Note: PC likely underestimates true gradient if significant collateral decompression is present

docs.title: CMR Imaging
docs.text:
Morphology: - Axial dark blood imaging to visualize narrowing - Sagittal reconstructions to measure coarctation shelf - Note bicuspid aortic valve (present in ~50% of CoA)

Flow assessment: - PC velocity at narrowest point - Mild: <2 m/s - Moderate–severe: >2 m/s (Bernoulli: ΔP = 4V² = >16 mmHg) - Compare proximal (ascending aorta) vs distal (descending aorta) flow

Aortic root/ascending aorta: - Measure diameter at annulus, sinuses, sinotubular junction - Aortic root dilation common and progressive

docs.title: Associated Findings
docs.text:
• Bicuspid aortic valve: ~50% association; assess for stenosis or regurgitation
  • Hypoplastic features: Narrowed aortic arch, underdeveloped descending thoracic aorta
  • Turner syndrome: Increased CoA coexistence; screen for other cardiac/renal abnormalities
  • Mitral valve abnormalities: Hypoplastic MV
  • Ventricular septal defect: May coexist

docs.title: Surgical Repair (End-to-end anastomosis or patch)
docs.text:
• Re-coarctation: Narrowing at repair site; occurs in 10–20% over years
  • Aneurysm: Especially at Dacron patch sites; risk of rupture
  • Aortic root changes: Continued progression of dilation despite successful repair

docs.title: Balloon Expandable Stent Placement
docs.text:
• Effective for postoperative re-coarctation
  • Complications: Residual narrowing, aortic wall perforation, stent fracture

docs.title: Hypertension Management
docs.text:
• Pre-repair: Often severe hypertension; medications required
  • Post-repair: Hypertension may persist even after successful repair due to aortic stiffness
  • Long-term monitoring essential (home BP checks, antihypertensive therapy)

docs.title: Imaging Follow-up
docs.text: Serial imaging (CMR or CT) to assess: - Residual narrowing and peak velocity - Aortic root growth - Development of collaterals (if re-narrowing) - Associated lesion progression (e.g., aortic stenosis)

docs.title: Double-Outlet Right Ventricle (DORV)
docs.text: Key anatomy/defining feature: Both great arteries arise entirely or predominantly (>50% each) from the morphologic RV, with physiology spanning VSD → TOF → TGA → single ventricle depending on VSD location and associated lesions.

docs.title: Anatomy & Classification
docs.text:
The VSD position relative to the great arteries determines physiology and surgical strategy:

VSD Location Frequency Physiology (like) Typical Repair Subaortic ~50% VSD (no PS) or TOF (with PS) Intraventricular tunnel ± TOF-type repair Subpulmonary (Taussig-Bing) ~30% TGA ± CoA Arterial switch + VSD closure Doubly committed ~5% VSD ± PS Tunnel repair or arterial switch Remote (noncommitted) Variable Single ventricle Fontan pathway (occasionally biventricular)

Great artery relationships: Variable — posterior/rightward (normal spiral), side-by-side (d- or l-malposition), or anterior aorta. Absence of semilunar–AV valve fibrous continuity is a distinguishing feature from TOF or TGA.

Associations: Coarctation/arch hypoplasia (especially with subpulmonary VSD, up to 50%), AV valve straddling, ventricular hypoplasia, right atrial isomerism, 22q11 deletion, trisomy 13/18.

docs.title: Post-Repair CMR Assessment
docs.text: CMR assessment varies by repair type. Identify the surgical strategy before scanning.

docs.title: Measurements
docs.text: Measurement What to Assess Concern / Threshold RV EDVi / ESVi Volumetric assessment (cine SSFP SAx stack) RV dilation from PR, conduit regurgitation, or tunnel-related obstruction RV EF Systolic function <40–45% = significant dysfunction LV EDVi / EF LV size and function LV dysfunction from volume/pressure overload or tunnel geometry Pulmonary RF PC flow at MPA or branch PAs >20–25% moderate; >40% severe — threshold for reintervention RVOT / conduit gradient PC velocity mapping at narrowing Peak velocity >3–4 m/s suggests significant stenosis Subaortic tunnel gradient PC velocity through LV-to-Ao baffle Progressive obstruction common (5–50% reop rate) Branch PA flow split PC flow LPA vs RPA Differential perfusion from PA stenosis Aortic root dimensions Sinuses, STJ, ascending Ao Dilation possible post–arterial switch Neoaortic regurgitation PC flow / cine More common in DORV post–arterial switch vs standard TGA QRS duration (ECG) Correlate with CMR >180 ms = arrhythmia risk (TOF-type repairs)

docs.title: Intraventricular Tunnel Repair (Subaortic / Doubly Committed VSD)
docs.text:
• Subaortic obstruction: Measure peak velocity through the LV-to-Ao tunnel on PC velocity mapping. Evaluate tunnel geometry on cine SSFP (axial and coronal oblique). Progressive narrowing is a major long-term concern.
  • Residual VSD / tunnel leak: Look for residual shunting around the baffle (Qp:Qs by PC flow). SSFP cine and LGE may delineate patch position.
  • RVOT obstruction: Assess subpulmonary/valvular region if infundibular septum was resected or PS was addressed.

Subaortic Stenosis Risk

Complex intraventricular tunnels carry the highest risk of progressive subaortic obstruction (up to 50% reintervention in subpulmonary VSD tunneled to aorta). Serial measurement of tunnel gradient is essential.

docs.title: TOF-Type Repair (Subaortic VSD + Pulmonary Stenosis)
docs.text:
• Pulmonary regurgitation: Quantify PR fraction by PC flow at MPA. Drives RV dilation — same PVR thresholds as TOF (RVEDVi >150–160 mL/m² and/or RF >40%).
  • Residual RVOT obstruction: PC velocity at site of narrowing; assess for transannular patch or infundibular resection adequacy.
  • RPA/LPA stenosis: Compare branch PA flows; evaluate with MRA or 3D SSFP.

docs.title: Arterial Switch (Subpulmonary VSD / Taussig-Bing)
docs.text:
• Neoaortic root and valve: Assess for progressive neoaortic regurgitation (more common in DORV than standard TGA). Measure root dimensions at annulus, sinuses, STJ.
  • Coronary artery patency: Evaluate on coronary MRA or stress perfusion if clinically indicated.
  • Supravalvular PS (neo-RVOT): Common at PA anastomotic sites — PC velocity mapping.
  • Aortic arch: Screen for residual coarctation or arch hypoplasia (sagittal oblique cine + PC flow in descending aorta; collateral flow in chest wall vessels).

docs.title: Rastelli / REV / Nikaidoh (TGA-type + PS)
docs.text:
• RV-PA conduit: Measure conduit gradient (PC velocity) and regurgitation. Conduit degeneration is inevitable — serial monitoring required.
  • Subaortic tunnel patency: Same concerns as tunnel repair above.
  • RV dilation/dysfunction: REV procedure avoids conduit but may cause significant RV dilation.

docs.title: Fontan Pathway (Remote VSD / Single-Ventricle Palliation)
docs.text:
• Fontan pathway flow: Assess for obstruction at cavopulmonary connections (cine + PC flow). Look for fenestration patency.
  • Systemic ventricular function: Single RV function (SSFP volumetrics).
  • AV valve regurgitation: Quantify by PC flow (aortic forward flow minus ventricular stroke volume).
  • PA size and flow distribution: MRA for pulmonary arterial anatomy.
  • Hepatic assessment: Liver congestion — note hepatic vein flow patterns.

Tailor the CMR Protocol to the Repair

DORV encompasses virtually the entire spectrum of CHD physiology. Always review the operative report before scanning. A "DORV" patient may have TOF-like physiology, TGA-like physiology, or Fontan circulation — each requiring a different CMR protocol focus.

docs.title: 3D Imaging & Advanced Techniques
docs.text:
• 3D SSFP / MRA: Essential for delineating spatial relationships of great arteries, tunnel geometry, conduit course, and PA anatomy.
  • 4D flow: Useful for comprehensive assessment of multiple flow jets (tunnel, conduit, branch PAs, neoaortic regurgitation) in a single acquisition.
  • LGE: Assess for myocardial fibrosis at patch sites, tunnel borders, and ventricular myocardium (prognostic for arrhythmia).
  • 3D printing: CMR-derived models may aid preoperative planning for complex re-repairs — particularly for "routability" assessment of remote VSDs.

docs.title: Indications for Reintervention
docs.text: Indication Threshold / Trigger Severe PR with RV dilation (TOF-type) RVEDVi >150–160 mL/m², RF >40%, declining RV EF Subaortic obstruction (tunnel) Progressive gradient, symptoms, LV dysfunction Conduit stenosis/regurgitation Peak gradient >50 mmHg (echo) or severe regurgitation with RV dilation; consider percutaneous PV Neoaortic regurgitation (arterial switch) Progressive AR with LV dilation Residual coarctation Gradient >20 mmHg with collaterals or hypertension Fontan pathway obstruction Increasing cyanosis, exercise intolerance, protein-losing enteropathy

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
• Subaortic obstruction — especially after complex tunnel repairs
  • Subpulmonary / conduit obstruction and regurgitation
  • AV valve regurgitation — may worsen from intracardiac repair geometry
  • Neoaortic regurgitation — post–arterial switch (higher incidence than standard TGA)
  • Coarctation / recoarctation
  • RV dilation and dysfunction — conduit regurgitation, PR, or post-REV
  • Arrhythmias — atrial (post-Fontan, AV valve disease) and ventricular (complex tunnel, TOF-type repairs)
  • Sudden cardiac death — risk with prolonged QRS, severely stenosed conduits, ventricular dysfunction
  • Heart block — post-VSD closure (conduction system at risk near TV annulus/crux)
  • Endocarditis — particularly with prosthetic material, conduits, residual defects
  • Thromboembolic events — especially Fontan patients

docs.title: Pregnancy
docs.text:
• Biventricular repair: Generally low risk if good biventricular function, normal saturations, and no significant residual hemodynamic lesions. Risk profile similar to TOF-type repair.
  • Fontan repair: Higher risk — comparable to other Fontan patients. Pre-conception CMR and specialist counseling mandatory.

Pre-Pregnancy CMR

All DORV patients contemplating pregnancy should have CMR to quantify biventricular function, valve competence, outflow tract gradients, and aortic arch status prior to conception.

docs.title: Imaging Follow-up
docs.text:
• Simple biventricular repairs (VSD-type, uncomplicated TOF-type): CMR every 2–3 years if stable; annual if borderline measurements.
  • Complex tunnel repairs: CMR every 1–2 years — high risk of progressive subaortic obstruction.
  • Conduit-bearing repairs (Rastelli, REV, Nikaidoh): CMR every 1–2 years to track conduit function; conduit replacement is inevitable.
  • Post–arterial switch: CMR every 2–3 years; more frequent if progressive neoaortic regurgitation or coronary concerns.
  • Fontan patients: CMR every 1–2 years with attention to ventricular function, pathway flows, AV valve, and hepatic assessment.
  • Escalate imaging for new symptoms, declining exercise capacity, new arrhythmias, or approaching intervention thresholds.

docs.title: Anatomy
docs.text:
Hallmark: Apical displacement of the septal and posterior leaflets of the tricuspid valve from normal insertion at the annulus.

Diagnostic criterion (echo): Displacement ≥8 mm/m² (body surface area) below the mitral valve annulus level

Key structural feature: - "Atrialized" RV: Portion of RV (from TV insertion to AV junction) is thin-walled and contractually incompetent; functions as part of RA - Functional RV: Small chamber; often hypertrophic and restricted

docs.title: Physiology
docs.text:
• Tricuspid regurgitation: Almost universal
  • Leaflets are dysplastic and may not coapt properly

  • Severity varies: mild to severe

  • Right-to-left shunting: ASD or patent foramen ovale present in ~70% of cases

  • Permits R→L shunting if RA pressure is elevated → cyanosis

  • Cyanosis typically mild (SaO₂ often 90–95%)

  • Atrial arrhythmias: Accessory pathways (WPW syndrome) in ~25% of Ebstein patients

  • Risk of accessory pathway-mediated tachycardia

docs.title: Ventricular Volumes and Function
docs.text:
• Measure functional RV volume (exclude atrialized portion from RV cavity)
  • Delineate atrialization boundary by cine
  • RV EF often reduced

  • Severe dysfunction may require intervention

  • LV volume and function: Secondary effects from RV dilation (septal bulge into LV)

docs.title: Tricuspid Regurgitation
docs.text:
• Severity assessment: 4D flow quantifies regurgitant volume and fraction
  • Pathophysiology: Dysplastic TV with malcoaptation; may have prolapse of leaflet

docs.title: Associated Findings
docs.text:
• ASD/PFO: Screen for right-to-left shunt on 4D flow
  • Accessory pathway: Consider electrophysiology referral if WPW pattern on ECG
  • Other structural abnormalities: LVOTO (left ventricular outflow obstruction), pulmonary stenosis

docs.title: Atrial Enlargement
docs.text:
• RA dilation: Often marked
  • LA size: Usually normal unless secondary LA dilation from TR

docs.title: Indications for Surgery
docs.text:
• Symptomatic cyanosis: SaO₂ <90%
  • Severe RV dysfunction: RV EF <35% or significant dilation
  • Progressive arrhythmias: Refractory to medical management
  • Paradoxical embolism risk: Large PFO/ASD with cyanosis

Surgical options: - TV repair (cone procedure) or replacement - ASD closure (to reduce R→L shunt) - Arrhythmia management (if WPW)

docs.title: Medical Management
docs.text:
• Antiarrhythmics for accessory pathway-mediated arrhythmias
  • Diuretics for right heart failure
  • Anticoagulation if atrial fibrillation develops

docs.title: Imaging Follow-up
docs.text: Serial CMR to assess: - RV volume and function trajectory - TR progression - LA/RA size changes - Development of arrhythmias or symptoms requiring intervention

docs.title: Physiology
docs.text:
Goal: Eliminate the subpulmonary ventricle; direct systemic venous return directly to pulmonary arteries (passive flow).

Types: - Atriopulmonary connection: RA to PA (older, rarely used now) - Total cavopulmonary connection (TCPC): IVC and SVC → PA (standard modern approach, often with fenestration)

Staged approach: 1. Norwood procedure (if needed): Restructure aorta; RV-to-PA shunt 2. Glenn procedure (Stage 2): SVC to PA (hemi-Fontan) 3. Fontan procedure (Stage 3): IVC to PA, completing TCPC

docs.title: Critical Parameters
docs.text:
• Single (systemic) ventricle EF and EDVi: Primary prognostic marker
  • EF >40% is favorable

  • Progressive decline predicts worse outcomes

  • Fontan conduit flow: Total superior + inferior systemic venous return

  • Compare to pulmonary artery flow to assess collateral flow

  • Pulmonary artery flow distribution:

  • LPA flow
  • RPA flow

  • LPA:RPA ratio (aim for ≥0.4 for balanced distribution)

  • Pulmonary collateral flow: Calculated as (Total PA flow) − (Fontan conduit flow)

  • Normal: <20% of systemic cardiac output

  • Significant collaterals (>20%): Bypass pulmonary vascular bed, reduce PA flow

  • Systemic venous collaterals: Bypasses Fontan circuit

  • Porto-systemic collaterals (suggest elevated IVC pressure)
  • Scan to include abdomen/inguinal region

docs.title: AV Valve Function
docs.text:
• Atrioventricular valve regurgitation: Common and progressive
  • Quantify regurgitant fraction by 4D flow
  • Severe regurgitation increases risk of failure

docs.title: Additional Assessments
docs.text:
• Ventricular function and fibrosis: LGE helps identify early remodeling
  • Pulmonary vascular resistance markers: Elevated Fontan pressure correlates with elevated collateral flow
  • IVC flow pattern: Retrograde flow suggests elevated IVC pressure (poor prognosis marker)

docs.title: Fenestration
docs.text: Intentional R→L shunt: Small connection between Fontan conduit and RA (or fenestration in baffle). - Allows decompression of elevated venous pressure - Trade-off: Mild cyanosis (lower SaO₂, typically 92–95%) - CMR findings: Expect Qp:Qs <1.0; visible shunt on 4D flow

docs.title: Long-term Complications
docs.text:
• Protein-losing enteropathy (PLE): Albumin loss from intestinal lymphatic obstruction
  • Risk factors: Low EF, elevated Fontan pressure, severe collaterals
  • Atrial arrhythmias: Intra-atrial reentrant tachycardia (IART) common
  • Thromboembolic risk: Consider anticoagulation if persistent atrial fibrillation
  • Progressive ventricular dysfunction: EF decline over years to decades
  • Hepatic cirrhosis: Chronic venous congestion → fibrosis

docs.title: Imaging Follow-up
docs.text: Serial CMR (typically yearly in young children, varies by stability) to track: - Ventricular volume and function - Growth of collateral vessels - Progression of valve disease - Signs of protein-losing enteropathy

docs.title: D-TGA: Post-Arterial Switch (Jatene Procedure)
docs.text: Physiology: Aorta arises from right ventricle, pulmonary artery from left ventricle. Arterial switch repositions great vessels to anatomically correct positions.

docs.title: Post-repair Assessment
docs.text:
Neo-aortic root dilation: The native pulmonary artery (now functioning as aorta) commonly dilates. - Measure at annulus, sinuses of Valsalva, sinotubular junction - Progressive dilation may warrant intervention

Neo-pulmonary stenosis (RVOTO): - Right ventricle now pumps to pulmonary artery (behind aorta) - Narrowing can develop at anastomotic site - Assess by PC velocity; use Bernoulli equation

Coronary anatomy: - Native coronaries are re-implanted into neoaortic root - Assess for ostial stenosis (best by coronary CT or invasive catheterization)

Ventricular function: - LV is now systemic ventricle (normal location) - May show functional decline if post-ischemic complications

docs.title: D-TGA: Post-Atrial Switch (Mustard/Senning Procedure)
docs.text:
Note: This is older operative approach; fewer cases in modern practice but important for long-term follow-up

Physiology: Intra-atrial baffle redirects inferior/superior vena cava to mitral valve and pulmonary veins to tricuspid valve. Systemic RV: RV remains systemic ventricle; bears pressure load chronically.

docs.title: Long-term Assessment (CMR focus)
docs.text:
Systemic RV volume and function: - RV EF and EDV critical prognostic markers - EF <40% indicates significant dysfunction - Progressive EF decline predicts worse long-term outcomes

Baffle complications: - SVC baffle stenosis (obstruction of superior vena cava pathway) - IVC baffle stenosis (obstruction of inferior vena cava pathway) - Baffle leak: R→L shunting allows deoxygenated blood to systemic circulation

AV valve regurgitation: - Systemic (tricuspid) regurgitation from chronic RV dilation

Arrhythmias: High incidence of atrial fibrillation and flutter due to stretched atria

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text: Long-term follow-up includes echocardiography and periodic CMR to assess RV decline and baffle integrity.

docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF)
docs.text: Four components: VSD + overriding aorta + RVOTO + RVH

docs.title: Pre-repair
docs.text: Early diagnosis via fetal echo or postnatal echocardiography. Surgical candidates typically have adequate pulmonary arteries and left-to-right shunting with cyanosis.

docs.title: CMR Evaluation
docs.text: Measurement Normal Concern RV EDVi 80–110 mL/m² >160 suggests progressive dilation RV EF >45% <40% = significant dysfunction Pulmonary RF <20% >40% = severe PR LV EF >55% QRS duration <160 ms >180 ms = arrhythmia risk

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Pulmonary regurgitation: Measure PR by 4D flow at main PA. Progressive PR leads to RV dilation; major reason for pulmonary valve replacement (PVR)
  • Residual RVOTO: Measure by PC velocity at site of narrowing (often infundibular). Use Bernoulli equation
  • RPA stenosis: Common post-repair. Compare LPA:RPA flow ratios
  • Aortic root dilation: Common sequelae. Measure at annulus, sinuses of Valsalva, sinotubular junction, ascending aorta

docs.title: PVR Timing
docs.text: CMR RV volumes guide PVR indication. Most centers use: - RVEDVi >150–160 mL/m² AND/OR RF >40% as thresholds for consideration of intervention

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
• Arrhythmias (sustained VT risk if QRS >180 ms)
  • Aortic root aneurysm
  • Left ventricular dysfunction (late finding)
  • Sudden cardiac death risk in untreated severe RV dilation

docs.title: Classification
docs.text: Type Prevalence Location Features Perimembranous 80% Membranous septum Most common; can extend into inlet or outlet Muscular 5–20% Muscular septum May close spontaneously; varies by location Outlet (Supracristal) 5–8% Above pulmonary and aortic valves Risk of aortic regurgitation Inlet (Endocardial cushion) 8–10% Below AV valves Part of AVSD spectrum

docs.title: Hemodynamic Significance
docs.text:
Shunt magnitude from CMR 4D Flow:

Qp:Qs Category Clinical Significance <1.5 Small Hemodynamically insignificant; unlikely to need closure 1.5–2.0 Moderate May warrant monitoring; closure considered if progressive >2.0 Large Significant L→R shunt; closure indicated

docs.title: Special Considerations
docs.text:
Eisenmenger Syndrome: - Reversed shunt (Qp:Qs <1.0) and cyanosis from fixed pulmonary hypertension - Irreversible; closure contraindicated (would cause systemic RV afterload crisis) - Managed medically with pulmonary vasodilators

Aortic regurgitation: - Particularly with outlet/supracristal defects - Assess severity on cine imaging and by 4D flow

Associated lesions: - Concomitant ASD in primum type - Pulmonary stenosis (cyanotic form)

docs.title: CMR Assessment
docs.text:
• Defect location and size: Dark blood imaging (HASTE/TSE) axial stack
  • Flow calculation: 4D flow at pulmonary artery and aorta for Qp:Qs
  • Ventricular function: SAX cine stack of each ventricle
  • LV dilation: Secondary to volume overload from L→R shunt

docs.title: Closure Indications
docs.text:
• Qp:Qs ≥1.5 (Class IIa)
  • Qp:Qs >2.0 in children (Class I)
  • Pulmonary hypertension without reversal (Class I)
  • Absolute contraindication: Eisenmenger physiology

docs.title: Valve Grading Reference
docs.text: Quick-reference severity grading for stenosis and regurgitation. Based on ACC/AHA and ASE guidelines.

docs.title: Aortic Stenosis
docs.text:
Severity Vmax (m/s) Mean Gradient (mmHg) AVA (cm²) AVAi (cm²/m²) DI Mild 2.0–2.9 <25 >1.5 >0.85 >0.50 Moderate 3.0–3.9 25–39 1.0–1.5 0.60–0.85 0.25–0.50 Severe ≥4.0 ≥40 <1.0 <0.60 <0.25 Very Severe ≥5.0 ≥60 <0.6 — —

Low-Flow Low-Gradient AS

  • Classic LFLG: EF <50%, AVA <1.0 cm², mean gradient <40 mmHg → dobutamine stress echo
  • Paradoxical LFLG: EF ≥50%, AVA <1.0 cm², mean gradient <40 mmHg, SVi <35 mL/m²
  • CT planimetry of valve area helpful in discordant cases

AVA by continuity equation: \(AVA = \frac{CSA_{LVOT} \times VTI_{LVOT}}{VTI_{AV}}\)

docs.title: Aortic Regurgitation (CMR Grading)
docs.text:
Severity RF (%) RVol (mL/beat) ERO (cm²) Vena Contracta (mm) Mild <20 <30 <0.10 <3 Moderate 20–39 30–59 0.10–0.29 3–6 Severe ≥40 ≥60 ≥0.30 >6

CMR AR Assessment

Regurgitant fraction by 4D flow or 2D PC at the ascending aorta is the reference standard. RF ≥40% = severe. Also measure LV EDVi, ESVi, and EF.

Aortic root dilation thresholds by CT/CMR:

Site Normal Consider surgery Aortic annulus <25 mm/m² — Sinus of Valsalva ♂ <40 mm, ♀ <36 mm ♂ ≥55 mm (≥50 mm in Marfan/bicuspid) Ascending aorta <40 mm ≥55 mm (≥45 mm in bicuspid)

docs.title: Mitral Stenosis
docs.text:
Severity MVA — PHT (cm²) MVA — Planimetry (cm²) Mean Gradient (mmHg) PASP (mmHg) Mild >1.5 >1.5 <5 <30 Moderate 1.0–1.5 1.0–1.5 5–10 30–50 Severe <1.0 <1.0 >10 >50

Wilkins Score

Echocardiographic score for MitraClip/PBMV suitability: leaflet mobility, thickening, calcification, subvalvular thickening. Score ≤8 = favorable for PBMV.

docs.title: Mitral Regurgitation (CMR Grading)
docs.text:
Severity RF (%) RVol (mL/beat) ERO (cm²) Vena Contracta (mm) Mild <20 <30 <0.20 <3 Moderate 20–39 30–59 0.20–0.39 3–6.9 Severe ≥40 ≥60 ≥0.40 ≥7

CMR method: LV stroke volume − Ao net forward flow = MR regurgitant volume. Or direct: PC at mitral valve (less reliable due to valve motion).

docs.title: Tricuspid Regurgitation
docs.text:
Severity Vena Contracta (mm) ERO (cm²) RVol (mL/beat) RVSP Mild <3 <0.20 <30 Normal Moderate 3–6.9 0.20–0.39 30–44 Borderline Severe ≥7 ≥0.40 ≥45 Elevated Massive ≥9 ≥0.60 ≥60 —

RVSP estimation (TR jet): RVSP = 4 × V²TR + RAP

RAP estimate: 3 mmHg (normal IVC), 8 mmHg (borderline), 15 mmHg (dilated, non-collapsing IVC)

docs.title: Pulmonary Regurgitation (CMR Grading)
docs.text:
Severity RF (%) RVol (mL/beat) Mild <20 <30 Moderate 20–39 30–44 Severe ≥40 ≥45

Note

CMR RF by 2D PC or 4D flow at the main PA is the clinical reference for PR severity, especially in post-TOF repair follow-up.

docs.title: Pulmonary Stenosis
docs.text: Severity V<sub>max</sub> (m/s) Peak Gradient (mmHg) Mild <3.0 <36 Moderate 3.0–4.0 36–64 Severe >4.0 >64
